planners.name: NavFn
planners.description: Classic grid-based global planner using Dijkstra's or A* on the global costmap. Fast and reliable for 2D differential-drive robots.
planners.when_to_use: Default choice for most indoor robots. Use when you need fast planning and the robot does not have strict kinematic constraints (e.g., no minimum turning radius).
weaknesses: Not kinematically feasible for car-like robots

planners.name: SmacPlanner2D
planners.description: SE2 (x, y, theta) lattice planner using A* with analytic expansions on a 2D grid. Better path quality than NavFn at moderate cost.
planners.when_to_use: When NavFn produces low-quality paths in complex environments. Good drop-in upgrade from NavFn for differential-drive robots.
weaknesses: Still not suitable for car-like kinematics

planners.name: SmacPlannerHybrid
planners.description: Hybrid A* planner for car-like (Ackermann) and legged robots. Plans kinematically feasible paths respecting minimum turning radius.
planners.when_to_use: Required for car-like robots, forklifts, and any platform with a non-zero minimum turning radius. Also preferred for precision docking on any robot.

planners.name: SmacPlannerLattice
planners.description: State lattice planner using pre-computed motion primitives for complex robot kinematics (omnidirectional, legged, etc.).
planners.when_to_use: When the robot has complex non-standard kinematics that cannot be captured by a single turning radius. Requires pre-generated lattice files.

planners.name: ThetaStarPlanner
planners.description: Any-angle path planner that produces straight-line paths between waypoints without being constrained to grid edges.
planners.when_to_use: When NavFn produces unnecessarily jagged paths in open spaces. Good for open warehouse or outdoor environments.
strengths: Smoother than NavFn in open space
weaknesses: Less optimal than Smac in complex environments

controllers.name: RegulatedPurePursuit
controllers.description: Geometric path-tracking controller based on pure pursuit with velocity regulation for curvature and obstacle proximity. The Nav2 default and recommended controller for most robots.
controllers.when_to_use: Default for most differential-drive and holonomic robots. Excellent balance of performance and predictability. Use when path tracking matters more than dynamic obstacle avoidance.
weaknesses: Does not optimize for dynamic obstacles like MPPI

controllers.name: MPPI
controllers.description: Model Predictive Path Integral controller. Samples thousands of trajectory rollouts and selects the optimal command. State-of-the-art for dynamic environments.
controllers.when_to_use: When the robot operates in dynamic environments with many moving obstacles. Requires more CPU than RPP. Best for robots with significant compute (x86 with GPU or modern ARM).

controllers.name: DWB
controllers.description: Dynamic Window Approach controller. Samples velocity commands in the robot's dynamic window and selects the best using configurable critics.
controllers.when_to_use: Legacy controller. Prefer RPP or MPPI for new deployments. Use DWB if you are migrating from ROS1 nav_core and want minimal changes.
weaknesses: Slower than RPP in open spaces
More complex to tune than RPP

costmap_layers.name: StaticLayer
costmap_layers.description: Loads a static occupancy grid from the map server and inserts it as the base costmap layer.
costmap_layers.when_to_use: Always present in the global costmap. Provides the pre-built map as the planning background.

costmap_layers.name: ObstacleLayer
costmap_layers.description: 2D obstacle layer that marks and clears cells based on 2D lidar or point cloud data using ray tracing.
costmap_layers.when_to_use: Standard choice for 2D lidar sensors. Used in both local and global costmaps for dynamic obstacle detection.

costmap_layers.name: VoxelLayer
costmap_layers.description: 3D voxel-based obstacle layer for processing RGB-D camera or 3D lidar point clouds. Projects 3D obstacles down to 2D.
costmap_layers.when_to_use: When the robot has a 3D sensor (Intel RealSense, Velodyne, Ouster). Captures obstacles that 2D lidar misses (low shelves, table legs).

costmap_layers.name: InflationLayer
costmap_layers.description: Expands obstacle cells with an exponentially decaying cost gradient. Keeps the robot away from obstacles without hard boundaries.
costmap_layers.when_to_use: Always present as the last layer in any costmap. The inflation radius and cost_scaling_factor are the most important safety tuning parameters.

costmap_layers.name: SpeedLimitLayer
costmap_layers.description: Applies speed limit zones from a speed limit map topic. The robot slows down in marked regions.
costmap_layers.when_to_use: When the environment has designated slow zones (pedestrian crossings, fragile floors, ramps). Requires a speed limit map publisher.

costmap_layers.name: RangeSensorLayer
costmap_layers.description: Inserts obstacles from ultrasonic or IR range sensors modeled as cone-shaped detections.
costmap_layers.when_to_use: For robots with ultrasonic bumpers or IR cliff sensors where 2D lidar coverage has blind spots (low obstacles, rear sensors).

recovery_behaviors.name: Spin
recovery_behaviors.description: Rotates the robot in place by a commanded angle to find a new escape path when stuck.
recovery_behaviors.when_to_use: Default recovery when the robot is stuck with a blocked forward path. The BT typically tries this before backup.

recovery_behaviors.name: BackUp
recovery_behaviors.description: Drives the robot in reverse by a commanded distance to free itself from a stuck position.
recovery_behaviors.when_to_use: Second recovery attempt after spin. Clears the robot from a position blocked on all forward sides.

recovery_behaviors.name: DriveOnHeading
recovery_behaviors.description: Drives forward on the robot's current heading by a commanded distance.
recovery_behaviors.when_to_use: Recovery when the robot needs to move slightly forward to clear a stuck position before replanning.

recovery_behaviors.name: Wait
recovery_behaviors.description: Waits in place for a commanded duration. Used in BTs to pause before re-attempting navigation.
recovery_behaviors.when_to_use: Final recovery attempt or when dynamic obstacles are expected to clear. Prevents rapid retry loops.

recovery_behaviors.name: AssistedTeleop
recovery_behaviors.description: Filters and forwards teleop commands through collision checking. Allows manual control while preventing collisions.
recovery_behaviors.when_to_use: In semi-autonomous deployments where an operator can take over during stuck situations. Add to behavior_plugins list.

smoothers.name: SimpleSmoother
smoothers.description: Iterative gradient-descent path smoother. Minimizes a weighted combination of smoothness and path fidelity.
smoothers.when_to_use: Default choice. Works well with NavFn and ThetaStar planners to remove grid artifacts from paths.

smoothers.name: ConstrainedSmoother
smoothers.description: Optimization-based smoother using CasADi/IPOPT that respects kinematic constraints (curvature limits).
smoothers.when_to_use: When SmacPlannerHybrid paths need further post-processing to respect turning radius constraints. More accurate but significantly slower than SimpleSmoother.
